title: Conjecture 1
statement_text: For every simple graph of order $n$, the expected number of faces when selecting an orientable embedding of $G$ uniformly at random is $O(n)$.
context_text: Stahl proved in 1991 that $E[F] \leq n\log n$ for any simple graph on $n$ vertices. Many families of graphs achieve $E[F] = \Theta(n)$, but no examples with super-linear expected face count were known, motivating this conjecture.
attributed_to: Halasz, Masarik, Samal, Campion Loth, and Mohar
paper_contribution: Proved as a corollary of Theorem 3, which establishes $E[F] \leq n(H_{2\mu}+1)$; for simple graphs this specialises to the linear bound, further sharpened to $E[F] \leq \frac{\pi^2}{6}n$ by Theorem 8.
notes: PDF source; conjecture header reads 'Conjecture 1 ([1])'; authors of this paper are co-proposers of the conjecture via reference [1].
paper_title: Expected number of faces in a random embedding of any graph is at most linear

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: For every $n$-vertex multigraph $G$ with maximum edge-multiplicity $\mu \geq 2$, the expected number of faces when selecting an orientable embedding of $G$ uniformly at random is $O(n\log(\mu))$.
context_text: A generalisation of Conjecture 1 to multigraphs. Tightness is witnessed by a chain of $n/2$ dipoles each with $\mu$ parallel edges joined by cut edges, which satisfies $E[F] \geq \frac{1}{2}n(H_{\mu}-1) = \Theta(n\log\mu)$.
attributed_to: Halasz, Masarik, Samal, Campion Loth, and Mohar
paper_contribution: Proved as a corollary of Theorem 3: $E[F] \leq n(H_{2\mu}+1)$, and since $H_{2\mu} \sim \log(2\mu)+\gamma$, this gives $O(n\log\mu)$; the bound is tight up to a constant factor.
notes: PDF source; conjecture header reads 'Conjecture 2 ([1])'; authors of this paper are co-proposers of the conjecture via reference [1].
paper_title: Expected number of faces in a random embedding of any graph is at most linear

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: For any simple graph $G$ of order $n$, $E[F] \leq \frac{1}{3}n + 1$.
context_text: The paper proves $E[F] \leq \frac{\pi^2}{6}n$ for simple graphs (Theorem 8), but no examples approaching this constant are known. A chain of triangles connected by cut edges achieves $E[F] = \frac{1}{3}n+1$, which the authors conjecture is the true optimal bound.
notes: PDF source; fraction $\frac{1}{3}$ appears as '1\n3' in raw PDF text but context is unambiguous.
paper_title: Expected number of faces in a random embedding of any graph is at most linear
